Agentic Ai

Programming

LLM

ChatGPT

Automation

10 min read
Just now

--

Your ‘AI Agent’ Is Probably Just a Chatbot With Extra Steps
AGENTS.md, Skills, MCP, A2A, Sub-Agents — the five words that separate a demo from a system you can trust with real work.
Here are two products. Both are sold as “AI agents.” Both run on the exact same language model.
The first one takes a prompt, calls an API, returns an answer, and stops.
The second one knows the rules of its environment, picks the right procedure, pulls data from three business systems, hands part of the job to a specialist, checks the result, and escalates when something looks wrong.
Same model. Completely different machine.
One is an LLM with a tool bolted on. The other has something closer to an operating architecture — and in 2026, that gap is the whole story.
Because companies are done with demos where an agent books a fake flight or looks up the weather. They want agents inside real processes: contracts, invoices, approvals, permissions, money. And that is exactly where the thin version falls apart:

• The agent can reach the accounting system, but doesn’t know the approval policy.

• It knows the policy, but not how to run a three-way match.

• It can pull the invoice, but needs a procurement specialist to explain a discrepancy.

• The file is 300 pages, too big for one context window.

• Two agents from two vendors have to cooperate, and don’t speak the same language.
None of these are model problems. They’re architecture problems. And five terms have emerged to name the missing pieces.
5 AI Agent Terms You MUST Know to Keep Up in 2026Prefer watching? The video walks through all five visually and shows how they click together. The article goes deeper.

The five pieces, in one picture
The terms are AGENTS.md, Agent Skills, MCP, A2A, and Sub-Agents. Thrown into the same slide, they look like five more AI acronyms that mean roughly the same thing. They don’t. Each solves a different missing part.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The cleanest way to feel the difference: imagine onboarding a new employee. Before they can do anything useful, five things have to be clear.

• Which rules must they follow? → AGENTS.md

• Which procedures do they know? → Agent Skills

• Which systems can they touch? → MCP

• Which colleagues can they call on? → A2A

• Which work can they hand off? → Sub-Agents
The model is the raw intelligence of that new hire. Necessary, but on day one, useless without the other five. Let’s walk one real task, a supplier invoice, straight through the stack.

1. AGENTS.md — so the agent stops guessing
Give a new developer a big codebase and say “fix the auth bug.” The sentence is clear; a dozen unwritten rules are not. Which package manager? Which test command? Which files are off-limits? Which security rules apply?
A human digs through docs and asks around. A coding agent hits the same wall, then confidently makes the wrong change because nobody told it the local rules.
AGENTS.md is the fix: an open Markdown file that gives agents one predictable place to find project-specific instructions — build commands, test requirements, conventions, security rules. A README written for machines instead of people.
## Development
Use pnpm. Run all unit tests before finishing a task.## Code style
snake_case for variables. No new dependencies without approval.## Security
Never touch production credentials.
Never log personal customer data.None of this makes the model smarter. It makes it reliable, and for real work, reliable beats smart.
The idea reaches far past coding. Our invoice agent needs standing rules too: never approve without supporting documents, always keep the original, never send financial data to an external model, escalate anything above a threshold, demand human review when a supplier’s bank details change. These aren’t task instructions you retype every time. They’re the company handbook, and the agent should read it before it starts, not rediscover it every run.
The rule of this layer: an agent needs a stable understanding of its environment before it acts inside it.

2. Agent Skills — knowing the rules isn’t knowing the job
Now the agent knows the rules. Then you ask: “Should this invoice be approved?”
Rules don’t answer that. Which documents get compared? Which price gaps are tolerable? What if the supplier name matches but the bank account doesn’t? Those are procedures, not policies.
An Agent Skill packages a reusable procedure — a process, reference material, templates, scripts — for one kind of work. The key move: a skill loads only when its task shows up, so heavy procedural detail stays out of the context until it’s actually needed. (Claude Code’s skills work exactly this way, following the open Agent Skills standard.)
A standing rule says “never approve without sufficient evidence.” The three-way-matching skill defines what “sufficient” means and how to prove it:

• Find the invoice, its purchase order, and the goods receipt.

• Compare supplier, numbers, dates, quantities, prices, tax, totals.

• Apply tolerance rules; catch duplicates; flag missing docs.

• Produce a structured explanation; route exceptions to a human.
Why not just dump every procedure into one giant system prompt? Because an agent carrying invoice rules and contract analysis and HR and compliance and deployment steps into every task drowns in irrelevant instructions, burns context, and starts contradicting itself. Skills turn expertise into modular capability: the contract skill loads for a contract, the invoice skill for an invoice, and nothing else gets in the way.
AGENTS.md tells the agent how to behave. Skills tell it how to do a specific job.

3. MCP — a brain with no hands can’t work
The agent understands the environment and has loaded the matching skill. It knows precisely how to validate this invoice.
It just can’t see the purchase order. Can’t reach the delivery record. Can’t query the supplier database. Can’t check whether the invoice was already paid. It knows what to do and has no way to do it.
MCP, the Model Context Protocol, is the standard that gives it hands. An open protocol that lets AI applications connect to files, databases, tools, APIs, and workflows through one common interface. The official line calls it “a USB-C port for AI”: one connector instead of a custom cable per system.
search_purchase_orders
retrieve_supplier_record
create_approval_taskThe agent discovers these tools, learns their inputs, calls them, and gets structured results back. Two things people constantly get wrong:
MCP doesn’t replace APIs. Your ERP, DMS, and CRM keep their existing APIs. MCP adds a standardized, agent-facing layer on top, so many AI clients can reuse one integration instead of everyone building their own connector.
MCP doesn’t make an agent. An MCP server exposes a search or an action — it doesn’t decide when to use it, whether the result is good enough, or what comes next. The agent decides. A calculator isn’t an accountant; an ERP connector isn’t a procurement agent.
And the real question shifts fast. It starts as “how do we connect the agent?” and becomes “which actions should it be allowed to take?” Reading an invoice is not approving the payment. Retrieving a customer record is not seeing every field in the CRM.
A powerful connection with no permission model isn’t enterprise architecture. It’s a security incident waiting to happen.

4. A2A — when the agent needs a colleague, not a tool
Our invoice agent finds something ugly. The invoice says 500 units. The goods receipt says 420. The purchase order looks like it was changed after delivery, and nothing on record says why.
No database call fixes this. The agent needs a specialist: a procurement agent that can read order history, or a warehouse agent that can inspect deliveries. That’s not agent-to-tool anymore. It’s agent-to-agent.
A2A, the Agent2Agent Protocol, standardizes how independent agents discover each other, exchange information, and collaborate as peers, even across different frameworks and vendors. The official framing is precise: MCP is how an agent talks to tools; A2A is how agents talk to each other.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

How does one agent know what another can do? Through an Agent Card — a machine-readable profile: identity, endpoint, capabilities, auth requirements, skills offered. A procurement agent might advertise “investigate PO discrepancies, verify supplier terms, analyze order revisions.” Our document agent reads that card and delegates the investigation instead of faking expertise it doesn’t have.
And, critically, A2A and MCP are not rivals. The document agent reaches the procurement agent over A2A. Inside its own work, that procurement agent uses MCP to query the ERP and read emails. A2A connects the agents; MCP connects each agent to its tools. Different layers, same building.

5. Sub-Agents — one agent shouldn’t carry everything
There’s a second way to overload a system: not by needing an outside agent, but by cramming too much into a single context.
Picture a 300-page supplier file: invoices, contracts, POs, delivery notes, emails, bank details, specs, handwritten notes. One agent could read it all, extract every field, compare every document, chase every discrepancy, and write the report. Technically possible. Architecturally a mess: the main context fills with raw text, dead-end theories, and intermediate math until the real instructions are buried.
Sub-agents split the load. Each is a specialist with its own context, instructions, tools, and permissions; it does one defined piece and returns only the conclusion to the parent. Anthropic describes them as task-specific assistants that keep the main context clean.

• Identification — which pages are which document?

• Invoice extraction — supplier, number, dates, line items, tax, totals.

• PO & delivery — retrieve and validate the order and the receipt.

• Duplicate check — has this invoice come through before?

• Risk — bank-detail changes, odd values, policy exceptions.
The parent doesn’t want every search result; it wants conclusions, confidence, evidence, and open issues. It stays focused on coordinating and deciding.
Sub-agent vs. A2A agent, since this is where people get lost: a sub-agent is part of the team the parent assembled. An A2A agent may belong to an entirely different team, vendor, or company. Sub-agents solve task division; A2A solves interoperability. A serious system uses both: internal sub-agents to process the documents, A2A to reach an external procurement or compliance agent.

The whole stack, on one invoice
Watch the five layers fire in order on a single, normal-looking invoice — right totals, known supplier, nothing obviously wrong:

• Standing rules load (AGENTS.md): required documents, thresholds, allowed systems, human-approval points, evidence to keep.

• The matching skill loads (Agent Skills): the three-way-match procedure, tolerances, exception logic.

• Evidence gets pulled (MCP): DMS, ERP, supplier DB, prior payments.

• Work splits (Sub-Agents): invoice, PO, delivery, identity, and duplicates checked in parallel.

• A specialist joins (A2A): the quantity mismatch goes to a procurement agent for investigation.

• The parent decides: it fuses extracted data, match results, policy, risk, and the external finding, then approves, rejects, or escalates within its permissions.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The five layers fire in order on a single invoiceThat’s not an LLM answering a question. That’s an orchestrated system doing controlled work.

The model is becoming the most replaceable part
For years the whole conversation was the model: biggest context window, top benchmark, best code, lowest price. Those still matter, but in production, the architecture around the model increasingly decides whether the thing is useful.
A stronger model can’t invent permissions it wasn’t given, retriev
